aliases: Minced prawn and pork rolls in beancurd skin

english: Minced prawn and pork rolls in beancurd skin

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for hae zho / prawn rolls in beancurd skin. Mapped to the closest defensible parent class 'deep-fried food product' (FOODON_03315595). A more specific term for Southeast Asian deep-fried seafood rolls is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans and products thereof — processed/prepared seafood product
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for hae zho. A0CKW (crustaceans and products thereof) is the closest defensible anchor given the primary prawn content. Beancurd skin wrapper and pork mince are secondary ingredients not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for hae zho or equivalent deep-fried prawn roll in beancurd skin as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of hae zho is 2 pieces (approximately 60 g), as commonly served at hawker centres and zi char stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn rolls (hae zho) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Prawn rolls (hae zho)' (CR ID: F03060020). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hae zho in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on analogous deep-fried mixed seafood and pork dim sum items with beancurd/wheat-based wrappers. The carbohydrate content (16.04 g/100g) is primarily from beancurd skin (tofu skin), which has a moderate GI profile similar to soy-based wrappers. Glycemic load per 60 g serving = (52 × 16.04 × 0.60) / 100 ≈ 5.0 g, classified as low GL. Estimation basis: University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for soy-based and mixed dim sum products. GI 52 is classified as low GI (55 or less).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 614 mg/100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-sodium threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit hae zho to no more than 1–2 pieces per occasion and avoid additional soy-based dipping sauces, as the sodium content of 614 mg/100 g alone represents over 25% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation increases AGE formation and estimated trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) impairs insulin sensitivity; 16 g carbohydrate/100 g with moderate fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit hae zho to 2 pieces per serving to control carbohydrate and fat intake, choose steamed or baked alternatives when available, and avoid pairing with high-GI dipping sauces, as the combination of deep-fried fat, AGEs, and moderate carbohydrate load can elevate postprandial blood glucose and worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (15.86 g/100 g), saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g), estimated trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g), and high sodium (614 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat hae zho as an occasional food only, limiting to 1–2 pieces per occasion, as the combination of deep-frying-derived trans fat, saturated fat from pork mince, and high sodium significantly increases LDL cholesterol, arterial inflammation, and blood pressure risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation (AGEs and trans fat) and pork mince (arachidonic acid) are pro-inflammatory; sodium 614 mg/100 g worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid hae zho due to deep-frying-derived trans fat and AGEs that promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity, and the arachidonic acid content of pork mince that amplifies inflammatory pathways; steamed or baked versions are strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hae zho (虾枣 / 蝦棗), also known as prawn rolls, is a classic Singaporean Chinese deep-fried snack and side dish made from a seasoned mixture of minced prawn and pork wrapped in dried beancurd skin (tofu skin) and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The name 'hae zho' derives from the Hokkien dialect, meaning 'prawn dates' — a reference to the cylindrical date-like shape of the rolls. The exterior is crispy and golden from deep-frying, while the interior is dense, savoury, and moist with a pronounced umami flavour from soy sauce, prawn, and pork. Hae zho is commonly served at hawker centres, zi char stalls, and during festive occasions such as Chinese New Year as part of a larger spread. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 262 kcal, 13.83 g protein, 15.86 g fat, and 16.04 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 614 mg. The deep-frying process increases total fat, generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), and introduces estimated trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g). These characteristics make hae zho a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. A typical serving is 2 pieces (~60 g). The beancurd skin wrapper contributes moderate dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g) and soy-derived protein, while the pork mince adds saturated fat and arachidonic acid. Hae zho is not halal due to pork content and contains crustacean and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: Is hae zho high in sodium?
What is hae zho made of?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database canonical entry for 'Prawn rolls (hae zho)' (CR ID: F03060020) with a stated confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat estimated at 3.8 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.3 g/100 g are derived from deep-fried pork and prawn composition analogues in USDA FoodData Central, as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this entry.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient, fatty acid fractions, and moisture data for this traditional deep-fried Singaporean preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. trans_fat_g is set to 0.3 g/100 g as an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central analogous deep-fried pork and seafood products; this is not a confirmed HPB-reported value. saturated_fat_g is estimated at 3.8 g/100 g from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries for deep-fried minced pork and prawn mixtures. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for soy-based and mixed dim sum products; no direct GI measurement for hae zho exists in the approved GI database. The input record incorrectly tagged this food with 'plant_based' in dietary_tags — this has been corrected as hae zho contains pork and prawn and is not plant-based. The input GI value of 0 with confidence 0.357 has been replaced with a defensible analogous estimate of 52 (low) per approved GI source policy. GI 52 is classified as low GI (55 or less).
